TextENGNew: ...Seven keys of varying sizes dangled from a keychain.
I had no way of knowing which was the right one, so I had to trust my instincts and try them one by one.
Statistically, I should hit the right key after three and a half attempts.
If I found it within the first three tries, that would be lucky.
Anything beyond that, and fortune was not on my side.
If I wanted to err on the side of caution, I should brace for the worst possibility -- getting the right key only on the seventh and final attempt.
A throbbing ache spread through my skull as composure and tension clashed within me.
Insert the key into the lock. Check if it turns.
All I had to do was repeat that simple procedure -- yet I couldn't stop the faint trembling in my fingertips.
...Tch... Inserting a key and turning it -- even a kindergartner can manage that much.
changeReason: JPN says 幼稚園児 (kindergartner) but original says 'three-year-old'; new translation corrects the age reference.

TextENGNew: ...So what does that make me right now? Less capable than a kindergartner?
changeReason: JPN says 幼稚園児以下 (less than a kindergartner) but original says 'more childish than a three-year-old'; new corrects this.

TextENGNew: Enough with the pointless thoughts. It doesn't matter if I'm clumsy.
...I just need to methodically try all seven keys...
...No matter how unlucky I am, the seventh key is guaranteed to work...
...But reality doesn't follow arithmetic.
The reality staring me in the face was devastating -- not a single key had worked.
I could feel the blood draining from my entire body with an almost audible rush.
...This wasn't the right set of keys?!
No -- the plate clearly indicated, albeit faintly, that these keys belonged to this lock.
...Had I even misread that?
Should I turn back now and carefully search through that key cabinet again...?
That would mean a catastrophic loss of time.
Every second I remained here, the risk accumulated further.
...In the very next second, some unforeseen disaster could strike and reduce everything to nothing...
If the only way to open this door was to go back to the administration office and search the key cabinet again... did I have any choice but to do it without hesitation?
...Then wasting even a few seconds sitting here in a cold sweat was the height of foolishness...!
The administration office being empty at this hour was a matter of probability, not certainty.
...Any staff member could wander back in on a whim.
And then I'd never get the chance to rummage through the key cabinet at my leisure...!!
...As if an electric shock had jolted through my crouching body, I sprang to my feet.
Precisely because panic was clawing at me, I needed to throw my body into motion to keep the anxiety at bay...
It was a response driven by pure human instinct.
I have to run back there as fast as I can -- it's dangerous...!!
Staying here is dangerous, and someone returning to the office is just as dangerous!
...I crushed that terror beneath a composure so cold it was almost painful -- frigid as dry ice...
Calm down, Shion...
...Mion would never lose her head over something like this...
Maybe I just made some kind of mistake...
Let me try the keys once more... just one more time...@k From the beginning... nice and slow...
Just then, the air filling the long, long corridor trembled, carrying the faint echo of someone's footsteps from somewhere far away.
Under normal circumstances, I would have recognized that the footsteps were extremely distant -- that there was no chance of encountering their owner.
...But in my current state of mind, such reasoning sounded like nothing more than sophistry.
Ahh... damn it...
I can't believe I'm this shaken up just from hearing distant footsteps.
Calm down... calm down...
I should savor this rush of adrenaline instead.
...Calm down, calm down.
...Just focus on the task in front of me, nothing more.
The distant footsteps pose no threat -- ignore them...
...Pretend they don't exist...!
I can't afford to miss something far more critical just because of footsteps that far away...!
Ahh, damn...
These keys... they were so hard and cold just moments ago, yet somehow...@k ...they've turned hot and rubbery in my hands,@k like they're made of soft putty...
How am I supposed to fit something this flimsy into a keyhole...?
Shit, shit, shit...!
Keys don't just go soft like that.
...It's my fingers that have gone limp.
...Ugh... shit, shit, shit...!
At that moment, the rubbery key sprang free, slipping through my fingers and clattering to the floor.
Spitefully, considering how impossibly pliable it had felt in my hands, you'd think it would land just as softly when it hit the floor--
But when it struck the ground, it produced a horrific noise, as though an entire china cabinet had been upended and smashed.
CLAAANNNGG!!! The tremendous crash threatened to burst my heart open.
That reverberating echo left my head reeling for a moment.
I froze rigid, then cautiously scanned my surroundings to check whether that thunderous sound had changed anything in my world.
Calm down, calm down...
Nothing's changed.@k I don't hear anything.@k ...So that means... I'm safe?
Calm down... calm down, calm down...
Wait... I don't hear anything?
What happened to those distant footsteps I heard before?
Calm down, calm down, calm down...!
Did they hear the keys drop and stop to listen -- trying to pinpoint where the noise came from...?
Ahh, damn... that dripping sound -- is it my own sweat spattering against the floor?
......Shut up, shut up, shut up...!
There's no way I could actually hear my sweat dripping...@k Calm down,@k calm down, calm down...@k CALM DOWN...!!!
Just to be clear,@k ...this place is neither a juvenile detention center nor a reformatory.
changeReason: JPN says 鑑別所/少年院 (juvenile detention center/reformatory) but original generalizes to 'prison or detention center'; new is more precise.

TextENGNew: It's a private academy run by a school corporation.
Not just any school, of course.
The entrance fee alone runs into the millions of yen.@k It's a boarding school offering an integrated education from elementary through university.
Add to that the fact that it's an all-girls institution... and it's less a school than a factory for producing docile hothouse flowers.
Some of my classmates have never even ridden public transportation -- and they mean it.
...It's anything but normal.
As far as I'm concerned, greeting people with 'How do you do' instead of 'Hey' is already absurd enough,@k and calling the teachers 'Sister' makes my skin crawl.
The morning and evening prayers are tedious, and I absolutely despise Sunday services.
Memorizing Bible passages is out of the question, and the mere mention of 'the spirit of charity' gives me hives!
changeReason: JPN says 慈愛の精神 (spirit of charity/compassion) but original mistranslates as 'Holy Ghost'; new correctly renders as 'spirit of charity.'

TextENGNew: If you lock someone up in this sort of institution -- this place I refuse to call a school -- for years on end, there are only two outcomes: you either shut down or you lose your mind.
changeReason: JPN says ふさぎこむかおかしくなる (become depressed or go crazy); original substitutes 'brainwashed' which is not in the JPN.

TextENGNew: Every other young lady here meekly chose to be indoctrinated... but someone as contrary as me could never accept that.

...After all, when I read the Bible, what impressed me was the strategic brilliance of religious warfare, the mechanisms of proselytization, and the international trade infrastructure built around the commodity called 'faith' -- never once was I moved by the love of God.
With an attitude like that, I'd been branded a problem child from the day I enrolled.
...Anyone could see how being treated that way would bring nothing but disadvantages.
Naturally, I caught on quickly enough, so I'd kept up the pretense of quiet obedience.
But at some point, maintaining that act grew tedious, and my true nature started bleeding through.
Problem child, round two.
The attitudes around me hardened rapidly.
Acting defiant certainly felt satisfying in the moment.
...But looking at the bigger picture, it was blindingly obvious that it did me no good whatsoever.
...So why did I keep it up?
Even knowing it would cost me later -- why this pointless rebellion?
...After subjecting myself to cold, rational self-analysis, I realized I had already reached my limit with this environment.
What I feared more than anything was being slowly smothered to death inside this languid greenhouse.
changeReason: JPN says 緩慢な温室の中で飼い殺される (kept confined to die slowly in a languid greenhouse); original reduces to 'brainwashed' which misses the greenhouse metaphor entirely.

TextENGNew: This is no place for someone like me to live.
I will not let myself be killed in a place like this--!
It was exactly six months ago that I resolved to break free -- to escape.
Strangely enough, the moment I made escape my reason for living, vitality flooded back into my days.
To throw the Sisters off my trail, I resumed my act of ladylike propriety.
Once I understood it as laying the groundwork for my ultimate objective -- escape -- the charade became exhilarating.
It was unbearably irksome when the Sisters, seeing me throw myself into volunteer work with apparent devotion, smiled benevolently and murmured something about the workings of God's grace.
Whenever they turned away, I stuck out my tongue behind their backs and grinned.
I volunteered for classmates' service duties and rotations so I could map the campus grounds, staff assignments, and security protocols.
...It didn't take long to discover that the security at this school -- entrusted with the daughters of prominent figures from every field -- was formidable.
Guards from a security company patrolled on random schedules, and the surveillance camera network blanketing the campus was ironclad.
The system designed to guard against intruders from outside had, ironically, become an equally effective barrier against an escapee from within.
But rather than deterring me... it only stoked the fire inside.
Over and over, I ran mental simulations of slipping through the surveillance net.
During class, I mapped escape routes in the margins of my notebooks, conducting meticulous tabletop exercises.
I identified the skills necessary for the escape and set about mastering each one.
All of it -- every last bit -- was thrilling.
...In the end, I suppose I'm simply the kind of woman who finds catharsis in this sort of thing.
I am who I am.
No amount of brainwashing will ever turn me into anyone else.
And so I watched and waited, searching for the moment when mind, skill, and body would all reach their peak together.
First: the courage and nerve to carry out the escape.
Those could only be forged upon a foundation of evidence and confidence in my plan's success.
To strengthen that foundation, I refined and honed my plan all the more.
Next: technique and physical stamina.
...I anticipated the obstacles along my escape route that would have to be overcome by force, and devoted myself to building my baseline fitness.
(Interestingly, once I embraced that mindset, even gym class became enjoyable.@k I even looked forward to the long-distance runs that everyone else dreaded.)
A flawless plan, and the physical strength and skill to execute it.
And undergirding it all, an unwavering resolve.
Once I confirmed that every element was in place, I set into motion the escape plan I had been carefully nurturing.
Before X-Day arrived, I had arranged for the school to be saddled with a particular kind of bomb.
changeReason: JPN uses 爆弾 (bomb) metaphor explicitly; original reduces to 'plant a seed' which loses the intensity of the original.

TextENGNew: That bomb was, in essence, the suggestion that one of the handful of male teachers on staff might be conducting secret trysts with a student.
I won't belabor the point, but in a girls' school of this caliber, such a thing would be a devastating scandal.
...The entire reason it's a girls' school in the first place is to shield these students from precisely that sort of trouble.
And the parents entrusting their daughters here are all prominent figures in their respective fields...
If it were true and became public knowledge... the headmaster hanging himself wouldn't even begin to suffice as atonement.
In a world of women, small rumors swell to enormous proportions.
Especially among girls of this age, brimming with curiosity about such matters yet forcibly isolated from any contact with the opposite sex.
changeReason: JPN says interest/curiosity about such matters while isolated from boys; original adds 'obsessed with dirty thoughts' which is not in the source.

TextENGNew: A rumor of this nature spread with breathtaking speed.
The Sisters scrambled to suppress it, warning the students not to be swayed by idle gossip.
But it was obvious to everyone that the ones most rattled by the rumor were the school administrators themselves.

By burdening the school with this bomb, I'd pushed them into a pattern: whenever any incident occurred, their instinct would be to handle it internally rather than contact families or the authorities.
And if I vanished skillfully under these conditions... it would look as though I had eloped with one of the teachers.
Naturally, every male teacher would deny it in unison.
And naturally, the school wouldn't believe them so easily.
...After all, rumors of illicit liaisons were already circulating on campus.

Eventually, the school would exhaust every other possibility and realize I had escaped on my own--@k but by that time, I'd have more than enough of a head start, already vanished beyond their reach...
I let the rumors permeate thoroughly, and then delicately hinted that the student involved in the secret meetings might be none other than me.
The Sisters interrogated me several times, but since the entire affair was fabricated from whole cloth, their investigation turned up nothing.
No matter how hard they pressed, all they found were alibis -- never a shred of evidence.
About three months after planting the rumors, I confirmed that the groundwork was sufficiently laid.
Then I personally swapped several after-dinner duties with classmates, engineering overlapping blind spots in the schedule for X-Day evening.
Requesting that someone cover for me 'just this one night' planted a tiny seed of suspicion in my classmates' minds.
That seed of doubt would, after my disappearance, blossom into the rumor that I had eloped with a male teacher.
And then, the night of X-Day arrived.
I excused myself for my various after-dinner duties and vanished from sight.
My disappearance would most likely be discovered at lights-out.
My generous roommate would probably wait about ten minutes before reporting me to a Sister -- but no longer than that.
The time I had was, at a generous estimate, just over an hour.
That was more than enough......!
I stepped off my designated route.
That single step was the opening act of my grand escape.
Calm... think calmly...
The keys I brought should be the right ones.
...This lock is just old and stiff.
Isn't it possible that I inserted the correct key but simply couldn't turn it because the lock was too stiff -- and I merely assumed it was the wrong one...?
I was well aware that my nerves were making me clumsier than usual.
These work gloves I was wearing to avoid leaving fingerprints were undoubtedly making my hands even more awkward.
...Let me try one more time, calmly...
...I selected the most likely candidate, inserted it into the keyhole once more... gently...
...Stiff.
...If I forced it, I worried the key itself might snap.
Just as I was about to give up -- I felt a satisfying click of resistance giving way.@k ...It opened!
I cracked the door open.
The chorus of insects I'd been hearing grew louder as it spilled through the gap.
There was no room for hesitation now.@k And yet, I felt a momentary reluctance to step outside.
Up to this point, I could still make excuses.
Desperate ones, perhaps -- but I could still talk my way out of this.
...Beyond this threshold, no such luxury existed.
Guards patrolled these grounds, and if they found me -- student or not -- I'd be hauled straight to the office.
There had apparently been escape attempts in the past.
...Well, of course there had.
I don't imagine I'm the only heretic here.
It made perfect sense that others before me had been unable to adapt to this environment.
Which is precisely why the school recognized student escapes as a real possibility.
...As for what happened to the students who were caught attempting to flee... I'd heard several rumors, none of which I wanted to believe.
...Every ghost story associated with this school seemed to involve a student who'd tried to escape.
...Damn it all... bring it on, then...
I wasn't naive enough to expect a second chance if I failed here.
...You only ever get one shot.
Beyond this door is where it truly begins...!!
I steeled myself... and pushed the door open.
The outside air hit me with a vivid, almost startling intensity.
Had the air I breathed every day always carried this powerful a scent...?
I'd never noticed such a thick, verdant, almost suffocating green smell before.
Was something abnormal happening out here...?
Should I be on guard?
Should I quietly return the keys and wait for another opportunity...?
...Don't be ridiculous.
Nothing was different from any other night.
This was the same air I always breathed.
...Just the ordinary scent of the outdoors that I'd never bothered to notice.
That's all it was.
For all my pretensions of cool-headed composure, I had a perfectly ordinary capacity for panic and anxiety...
...Get a grip.@k ...Get a grip, Shion...!
From here on, the map inside my head was my only weapon.
I'd located every surveillance camera by now.
...I was confident I could evade them all, but the real problem wasn't the cameras.@k It was the guards.
The guards' patrol times and routes were completely random -- impossible to predict.
This was an honest-to-god gamble.
Areas with cover were manageable.
But there was a stretch of several dozen meters where I'd be completely exposed.
changeReason: JPN says 約数十メートル (approximately several dozen meters); original says 'several meters' -- a significant difference in distance.

TextENGNew: I honed my focus and sharpened every sense.
...Well, when all is said and done, it comes down to luck in the end.
No matter how perfectly I executed my plan, one unlucky coincidence and it was over.
Conversely, even with the sloppiest plan imaginable, pure luck could carry me through unscathed.
...Heh. So all the effort I'd poured in up to now amounted to just that, huh?
Ahaha... I suppose this is what they call a gamble.
The moment I recognized what I was doing as a gamble, a thrill of excitement surged up from beneath the tension.
That's right -- this is a gamble.@k A test of fortune.
I, Sonozaki Shion, am about to stake everything on a trial of fate -- testing the resolve that drives me to transform my own life.
If my luck was so poor that I'd get caught escaping from a place like this, then even if I did somehow win my freedom, no decent future awaited me anyway.
But I'm different.
I'm stepping forward from here.@k I'm putting my luck to the test.@k Let's go... Shion!
I filtered out the droning insects and extracted only suspicious sounds.
...No signs of life.@k ...The loudest sound wasn't the insects -- it was my own heartbeat.
Crunch.
...Even my own footsteps were so deafening they threatened to burst my eardrums...
With every step, my overactive imagination tormented me with visions of stumbling upon a guard around the next corner.
Time and again, the agony became unbearable and I nearly broke into a run.@k ...But bolting in a panic would only produce a racket.
If someone heard running footsteps at a time and place where no one should be, anyone would realize something abnormal was happening.
But mere walking footsteps, even if overheard, would likely raise no alarm.
Of course, I had no intention of letting even those be heard.
I strained every sense, trying to gather more information than my field of vision alone could provide.
But no matter how intently I listened, I couldn't detect a single trace of another person.
...Paradoxically, I might have felt safer if I could at least sense a guard somewhere in the distance.
The total absence of sound, rather than reassuring me that my surroundings were safe, fed a gnawing worry that my senses were hopelessly dull and unreliable.
What if someone was right beside me... and I was simply too foolish to notice...?
The thought made me want to silence even my own footsteps, and I found myself stopping dead in my tracks from time to time.
...At this rate, it was no different from being frozen in place...
Stop or run.@k Something inside me demanded I choose one or the other.
Stopping was out of the question.
Moving forward carefully but swiftly -- anything less was meaningless.
...Every moment I remained here, the risk continued to accumulate.
And breaking into a run would be the height of folly.
...Ahh, I knew all that...!@k But the urge to run was agonizing to suppress.
My head was consumed by those thoughts the entire time I traversed the few dozen meters of exposed ground.
Eventually, without sensing a single soul, without encountering anyone at all, I made it through.
...I crouched down on the spot and couldn't help but expel the stale, rotten air that had filled my lungs...
...The negative emotions receded all at once.
...Almost there.@k ...Just a little more!
Through that thicket ahead lay the fence -- the barrier separating the campus from the outside world.
The urge to break into a sprint grew stronger by the second.
...I clamped down on it with every ounce of reason I had, and carefully approached the final obstacle......
The ornate Western-style fence encircling the campus was aesthetically impressive, but from a security standpoint, it left much to be desired.
I spent every moment terrified that someone would spot me in the vulnerable act of scaling it, but fortunately, no one did.
I was also plagued by the paranoid thought that the fence might be fitted with contact-triggered alarms.
...But it was too late for that now.
As I was already halfway up, indulging such fears served no purpose.
The drop from the top of the fence to the ground may have been a full two meters.
changeReason: JPN says ２ｍ (2 meters); original converts to 'seven feet' which is approximately correct but new keeps the original measurement.

TextENGNew: I'd climbed up carefully, so I should descend with the same caution.
...That was the logical conclusion, but it was also where my reason reached its limit.
Without a moment's hesitation, I chose to leap from that height.
Naturally, the instant I committed to freefall, I felt a twinge of regret.
That weightless, floating sensation unique to falling lasted far longer than I'd anticipated, and fear crept in mid-descent.
I didn't stick the landing -- I tumbled onto my backside.
But no one was there to laugh, and I had no room for sheepish embarrassment.
I scanned my surroundings to see whether the crash of my landing had drawn anyone's attention.
Even the insects, continuing their unbroken chorus, showed no interest in the sound I'd made. At last, I allowed myself to breathe...
I checked my watch.
...Five minutes to eight.
...The whole escape had taken just over fifteen minutes.
Reduced to a number, the brevity was striking.
Looking back on what I'd actually done during those fifteen minutes... the lion's share had probably been spent on anxious deliberation and second-guessing.
...Worrying over nothing, I suppose you'd call it.
But I couldn't let my guard down yet.
There was no guarantee I'd fooled every camera.
This place wasn't a bank, after all.
Even if a guard had caught a glimpse of my shadow, no alarm bell would start ringing.
For all I knew, guards were closing in on me right this very moment as I sat here dawdling.
It was still a bit too early to drink in the air of freedom.
I concealed myself in the darkness and waited.
The predetermined rendezvous time was almost here.
